entry.title: Rokarolla, FortiSandbox CVEs, DragonForce Teams Relays, and LiteSpeed cPanel Exploitation
entry.content:
Reporting in this window centered on active exploitation of exposed infrastructure, increasingly evasive malware tradecraft, and the continuing shift toward attack-path-based exposure management. Several items highlighted how attackers are blending legitimate services, anonymizing infrastructure, and trusted communication channels into intrusion workflows, complicating detection and attribution. Mobile banking malware, ransomware operations, phishing, cloud audit fidelity, and exploited appliance flaws all featured prominently. The coverage also reflected growing attention to continuous controls validation, operational resilience in healthcare, and AI-enabled analysis in OT environments. CISA added an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin vulnerability, identified in the reporting as CVE-2026-48172, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog. The flaw affects LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin versions before 2.4.8 and reportedly allows attackers with FTP or web shell access to escalate privileges to root on shared hosting servers running CloudLinux/CageFS. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure affected systems within three days under BOD 26-04 guidance. Fortinet FortiSandbox also appeared in exploitation reporting, with Defused Cyber observing activity against CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089. The first two issues involve JRPC API path traversal and OS command injection, both potentially reachable through crafted HTTP requests by unauthenticated attackers. CVE-2026-25089 was patched more recently, and Defused Cyber assessed that the observed exploit attempt for that flaw appeared AI-generated and faulty, with no working public exploit known. Zimperium documented Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps with support for 137 remote commands. The malware is distributed through malicious sites impersonating popular applications such as TikTok and Chrome, with an initial dropper posing as Google Play Protect. Once installed, it abuses Accessibility permissions to disable protections, capture lock-screen credentials, deploy fake login overlays, read and send SMS messages, log screens and keystrokes, and silently replace copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses. DragonForce ransomware was reported abusing Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure through a custom Go-based backdoor named Backdoor.Turn. Symantec described it as the first known in-the-wild malware to use Teams relay servers to mask command-and-control traffic. In a December 2025 intrusion against a major U.S. services company, the attackers likely gained access through an unknown SQL or MSSQL server flaw, used BYOVD techniques with multiple vulnerable drivers to obtain kernel-level privileges, exfiltrated data, and deployed ransomware. Business email compromise reporting from IRONSCALES showed a same-day payment request sent from a legitimate construction consulting domain via hosted Exchange infrastructure. SPF passed and DMARC returned bestguesspass, while the body fabricated a prior thread using real employee names and attached a clean PDF invoice with Wells Fargo banking details. The x-originating-ip header pointed to 45[.]67[.]97[.]6 in Seoul, inconsistent with the Albuquerque-based sender, and the message was mitigated before recipient action. A Spur Intelligence survey of more than 200 practitioners found anonymizing infrastructure, including VPNs and residential proxy networks, present in nearly every security incident. Nearly half of surveyed organizations reported significant operational or financial impact from account takeover attempts and credential abuse conducted through these services. Respondents also cited lack of IP context as a major analysis challenge, with many teams still using IP intelligence primarily after alerts are generated. Permiso highlighted risks around GCP’s deprecated serviceData field and the broader dependency of cloud detection engineering on audit log quality. The article emphasized that administrative activity in GCP is represented through audit log entries and that their structure and completeness directly affect detection rules, investigations, and post-incident reconstruction. The reporting framed log fidelity as a core constraint on reliable cloud threat detection. Tenable and XM Cyber coverage reflected the continued movement from traditional vulnerability counting toward continuous threat exposure management and attack-path prioritization. Tenable One’s continuous control validation maps defenses such as EDR, MFA, firewalls, SIEM, and DLP onto potential attack paths, while XM Cyber’s SC Awards Europe win was tied to attack simulation, attack graph analysis, and identification of choke points where a single remediation can disrupt multiple routes to critical assets. Radiflow described AI’s dual role in OT security: a force multiplier for attackers through deepfakes, automated phishing, faster scanning, and vulnerability chaining, and a defensive aid for asset visibility, threat intelligence, prioritization, and SOC efficiency. Its reporting focused on passive OT network learning through iSID and digital-twin-based breach-and-attack simulation through CIARA. In healthcare, Rady Children’s Hospital-San Diego CISO Sahan Fernando emphasized operational engagement as central to cyber resilience and continuity of patient care, citing sector-wide collaboration through Health-ISAC, the Health Sector Coordinating Council, and Operation Vital Signs.

Attackers Exploit Three Fortinet FortiSandbox Flaws, One Patched Last Week — Must Read

Attackers are actively exploiting three critical Fortinet FortiSandbox vulnerabilities, including one patched only last week, that can enable authentication bypass and unauthenticated command execution. Organizations using affected FortiSandbox products should apply Fortinet’s patches immediately.

  • Defused Cyber said it observed attackers exploiting CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 in Fortinet FortiSandbox over the past 24 hours.
  • CVE-2026-39813 is a FortiSandbox JRPC API path traversal vulnerability that could allow an unauthenticated attacker to bypass authentication using specially crafted HTTP requests.
  • CVE-2026-39808 is an operating system command injection flaw that could allow an unauthenticated attacker to execute unauthorized code or commands through crafted HTTP requests.
  • Fortinet patched CVE-2026-39813 and CVE-2026-39808 in April 2026, while CVE-2026-25089 was fixed last week.
  • Defused Cyber said the exploit for CVE-2026-25089 appears to have been developed using an AI model and is faulty, and no working exploit for that vulnerability has been publicly disclosed.

Ransomware gang abuses Microsoft Teams relays to hide malicious traffic — Must Read

DragonForce ransomware used a custom Go-based backdoor, Backdoor.Turn, to hide command-and-control traffic through Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure during an attack on a major U.S. services company. Symantec says this is the first known in-the-wild malware abusing Teams relays for C2, alongside sophisticated BYOVD and persistence techniques.

  • DragonForce ransomware used a custom Go-based malware named Backdoor.Turn to hide command-and-control traffic inside Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure.
  • Symantec says Backdoor.Turn is the first known in-the-wild malware to abuse Microsoft Teams TURN relay servers to mask command-and-control traffic.
  • The December 2025 attack against a major U.S. services company likely began with exploitation of an unknown flaw in an SQL or MSSQL server.
  • The attackers used BYOVD techniques with multiple vulnerable drivers, including Huawei’s HWAuidoOs2Ec.sys, Topaz Antifraud wsftprm.sys, Tower of Fantasy GameDriverx64.sys, and K7 Security K7RKScan.sys, to obtain kernel-level privileges and terminate security tools.
  • After reconnaissance and defense evasion, the attackers exfiltrated all data, deployed DragonForce ransomware, and encrypted the victim’s systems.

CISA warns of another cPanel plugin flaw exploited in attacks — Must Read

CISA ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel plugin flaw within three days. The vulnerability can let attackers with FTP or web shell access escalate privileges to root on affected CloudLinux/CageFS shared hosting servers.

  • CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure systems within three days against an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin vulnerability.
  • The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-48172 in the article, affects all LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin versions before 2.4.8.
  • Namecheap reported that the flaw allows attackers with FTP or web shell access to escalate privileges to root on shared hosting servers running CloudLinux/CageFS.
  • LiteSpeed released urgent security updates in early June and warned users to update the cPanel user-end plugin bundled with the WHM plugin.
  • CISA added the vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog and said agencies must follow BOD 26-04 guidance or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable.

New Rokarolla Android Malware Steals PINs, SMS Codes, and Crypto Wallet Funds — Should Read

Zimperium researchers found Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan spread through fake app sites that abuses Accessibility access to steal PINs, SMS codes, banking credentials, and crypto funds. Its 137 remote commands let attackers disable Play Protect, overlay fake login screens, intercept messages, rewrite crypto wallet addresses, and remotely control infected phones.

  • Zimperium's zLabs documented Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan that targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps and supports 137 remote commands.
  • Rokarolla spreads through malicious websites posing as well-known apps such as TikTok and Chrome, with an initial dropper pretending to be Google Play Protect.
  • The malware abuses Accessibility access to disable Google Play Protect, capture lock-screen PINs or passwords, deploy fake HTML login overlays, and take screenshots without using MediaProjection.
  • Rokarolla can read and send SMS messages, make itself the default app for texts and calls, block incoming calls, scrape contacts, read notifications, and log keystrokes and screens.
  • The trojan silently rewrites clipboard contents to replace copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses.

The Invoice That Originated from the Wrong Continent — May Read

A legitimate-looking invoice fraud email passed SPF and appeared to come from a real construction consulting domain, but header analysis revealed it was submitted from an anonymous Seoul IP inconsistent with the sender’s US-based business. The key takeaway is that authentication checks alone can miss payment diversion attempts when attackers use valid infrastructure and fabricated internal threads.

  • A same-day payment request arrived from a legitimate construction consulting domain via hosted Exchange infrastructure, with SPF passing and DMARC returning bestguesspass.
  • The email body fabricated a three-message thread using real employee names and directed the recipient to complete payment by the close of business.
  • The attached PDF invoice included line items, a payment amount, and Wells Fargo routing and account numbers, and static analysis found the PDF clean.
  • The x-originating-ip header showed 45[.]67[.]97[.]6, geolocated to Seoul, South Korea, with no PTR record, which was inconsistent with the Albuquerque-based sender.
  • Themis flagged the message at 62% confidence with a VIP Recipient label and triggered inline mitigation before the recipient could act.

GCP's serviceData: Officially Deprecated, Actively Dangerous — May Read

GCP’s deprecated serviceData field can undermine cloud audit log reliability, making detections and investigations risky if teams still depend on it. Security teams should update their logging and detection logic to use supported audit log fields instead.

  • The article states that GCP's serviceData is officially deprecated and actively dangerous.
  • Cloud audit logs are described as the backbone of detection engineering in cloud environments.
  • Security teams depend on cloud audit logs to identify suspicious behavior, build detection rules, and reconstruct attacker activity after an incident.
  • The article states that in GCP, every meaningful administrative action leaves a trace in the form of an audit log entry.
  • The quality of a GCP audit log entry directly affects the reliability of investigative and detection efforts.

Rady Children’s Fernando Says Operational Engagement Is the Key to Cyber Resilience — Optional

Rady Children’s CISO Sahan Fernando argues that healthcare cyber resilience depends on security teams deeply understanding hospital operations, not just deploying technology. He highlights broad sector collaboration through Health-ISAC, HSCC, and Operation Vital Signs to help ensure patient care can continue during cyber disruptions.

  • Sahan Fernando, Chief Information Security Officer at Rady Children’s Hospital-San Diego, says cyber resilience in healthcare depends on whether security teams understand how hospitals operate each day.
  • Fernando describes deep operational engagement as the foundation for keeping patient care running through disruption.
  • Fernando is participating in Operation Vital Signs and said he is excited to see how it is carried out with many participants across the United States.
  • Fernando serves on the Board of Health-ISAC and said there is strong interest across the health sector.
  • Fernando said Health-ISAC and the Health Sector Coordinating Council bring together providers, payers, pharmaceuticals, lab sciences, and others involved in patient care.

Survey: 94% of Incidents Involve Anonymized Infrastructure. Teams Are Still Reactive — Optional

A Spur Intelligence survey found that anonymized infrastructure like VPNs and residential proxies now appears in nearly every security incident, but many security teams still lack the context and workflows to act on IP data proactively. The key takeaway is that organizations need richer IP intelligence integrated into real-time risk decisions, not just post-incident investigations.

  • A Spur Intelligence study of more than 200 security practitioners found that anonymizing infrastructure, including VPNs and residential proxy networks, appears in nearly every security incident.
  • Nearly half of surveyed companies reported significant operational or financial impact from account takeover attempts and credential abuse conducted via VPNs and residential proxies.
  • Nearly half of respondents said lack of context is the biggest challenge for security teams analyzing IP activity.
  • Many organizations primarily use IP intelligence during investigations after alerts have already been generated, but respondents want more predictive and intelligence-led workflows.
  • A full third of companies surveyed are not measuring the effectiveness of their IP intelligence efforts at all.

The VM Category Shift: XM Cyber Wins SC Awards Europe 2026 Best Vulnerability Management Solution — Optional

XM Cyber won SC Awards Europe 2026 Best Vulnerability Management Solution, reflecting a shift from traditional scan-and-patch vulnerability management toward attack-path-led exposure management. The key takeaway is that teams should prioritize fixes based on whether vulnerabilities enable real paths to critical assets, not just severity scores.

  • XM Cyber won the SC Awards Europe 2026 Best Vulnerability Management Solution after Qualys had won the category for three consecutive years.
  • The SC Awards judges praised XM Cyber for clear differentiation from conventional vulnerability management tools through attack simulation, attack graph analysis, and CTEM-aligned methodologies.
  • The article says attack-path-led vulnerability management prioritizes vulnerabilities that sit on viable routes from initial access to critical assets and sets aside vulnerabilities that are dead ends.
  • The judges highlighted XM Cyber’s ability to identify choke points, where a single fix can disrupt multiple attack paths at once.
  • XM Cyber plans to add Discovery Scanning, Network Vulnerability Scanning, Cryptography Scanning, and Container and Cloud Workloads Vulnerability Scanning to its platform visibility pillars.

How AI Is Reshaping OT Security — Optional

AI is speeding up attacks against OT environments that cannot patch or change quickly, widening the gap between attacker speed and industrial operational constraints. Radiflow argues defenders should use AI to improve passive asset visibility, prioritize risk by operational impact, and reduce alert noise rather than simply adding more dashboards.

  • AI is acting as a force multiplier for attackers by enabling more convincing deepfake voices, personalized automated phishing campaigns, faster scanning for exploitable patterns, and quicker chaining of minor vulnerabilities into major breaches.
  • OT environments were built for stability rather than speed, with systems that run for decades, rare patching windows, and potentially costly unplanned shutdowns.
  • The article says effective AI use in OT defense should focus on clearer information through asset visibility, threat intelligence and prioritization, and SOC efficiency rather than adding more dashboards, alerts, and reports.
  • Radiflow’s iSID product passively learns the OT network from a mirrored data stream to build a live model of assets, links, vulnerabilities, and inter-device attack vectors.
  • Radiflow’s CIARA uses machine-learning-driven virtual breach-and-attack simulation to create a digital twin of the network, map vulnerabilities to business consequences, and produce prioritized mitigation recommendations.

Improving precision in CTEM: How continuous controls validation in Tenable One transforms exposure management — Optional

Tenable One is adding continuous control validation to help CTEM programs prioritize real, reachable attack paths by accounting for defenses like EDR, MFA, firewalls, and pentest results. The main takeaway is that teams can reduce alert noise and focus remediation on exposures that existing controls do not already block.

  • The article says organizations need to move from managing theoretical cyber risks to validating actual, accessible exposure because vulnerability exploitation is a leading initial access vector and frontier AI is accelerating vulnerability discovery.
  • Tenable One adds continuous control validation that maps active security controls such as EDR, MFA, firewalls, SIEM, and DLP onto potential attack paths.
  • Tenable One can deprioritize weaknesses that existing defenses already neutralize and help analysts filter top attack paths based on the presence of security controls.
  • The Tenable One Open Connector can ingest penetration testing results so organizations can layer real-world attack simulations over real-time exposure insights.
  • The article says combining control validation with asset criticality, threat activity, entitlement privileges, and attack pathways helps security teams focus on the most critical risks.
entry.summary: Reporting in this window centered on active exploitation of exposed infrastructure, increasingly evasive malware tradecraft, and the continuing shift toward attack-path-based exposure management. Several items highlighted how attackers are blending legitimate services, anonymizing infrastructure,...

entry.title: FortiSandbox, n8n, Cisco SD-WAN Exploitation and APT37 NarwhalRAT Activity
entry.content: During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally reachable workflow platforms, alongside regulatory pressure to produce incident context quickly. Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws were reported as under active exploitation, with Defused observing attacks against CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 in the prior 24 hours. Fortinet had issued fixes for the three critical-severity vulnerabilities on April 14; the flaws allow unauthenticated attackers to escalate privileges and execute unauthorized code via low-complexity command injection without user interaction. Fortinet also previously flagged CVE-2025-61624, a medium-severity path traversal issue, as exploited in the wild. Cisco released updates for CVE-2026-20262, an actively exploited vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager. The flaw stems from insufficient validation during file upload and can allow an authenticated remote attacker with write access to create or overwrite files, potentially escalating privileges to root. CISA added the issue to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with a June 29, 2026 remediation deadline for Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies. Cyera disclosed CVE-2026-21858, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability affecting locally deployed n8n instances. The issue arises from Content-Type confusion in Form webhooks, allowing attacker-controlled JSON to override trusted file metadata and copy local files such as n8n’s SQLite database or configuration into retrievable workflow data. The researchers described how those files could enable forged administrator sessions and subsequent command execution through n8n’s Execute Command node. CyCognito reported expanding external attack surface analysis to more than 60 AI stack components, including MCP servers, n8n, Ollama, MLflow, PyTorch, and Triton. The company described cases involving an exposed MCP server permitting unauthenticated natural-language access to CRM data and a public CrewAI stack where the agent API was authenticated but the RAG knowledge base remained exposed. Silent Push separately framed NIS2’s 24-hour early warning requirement as dependent on rapid infrastructure context, arguing that DNS, WHOIS, certificate, host scan, traffic, and behavioral relationship data are increasingly central to timely incident characterization. ESET reported Windows variants of SprySOCKS, previously associated with Linux malware, used between 2023 and 2024 against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras. The activity was attributed with high confidence to Earth Lusca, also tracked as FishMonger and known as Aquatic Panda, Red Dev 10, and TAG-22. The Windows variants, WINDRV and WINPLUS, support TCP, UDP, and WebSocket communications, more than 30 C2 commands, SOCKS proxying, process and file management, and collection of keystrokes, clipboard contents, and active window titles. Separately, Genians reported that North Korean group ScarCruft, or APT37, used spear-phishing emails impersonating Microsoft Account security alerts to deliver NarwhalRAT. The emails referenced abnormal one-time password activity and carried ZIP archives containing malicious LNK files, which initiated a multi-stage infection chain involving batch scripts, Python retrieval, and scheduled-task persistence. NarwhalRAT supports keystroke logging, screenshots, ambient audio recording, USB data collection, directory uploads, C2 command execution, and C2 switching via Korean website relays and pCloud API functionality. Kaspersky reported malicious Wallpaper Engine packages distributed through Steam Workshop since late 2025. The campaign abused Wallpaper Engine’s application wallpaper feature, which allows Windows-compatible programs to run as desktop backgrounds, and some malicious wallpapers had thousands or tens of thousands of downloads. One analyzed sample dropped the DarkKomet backdoor as Synaptics.exe, used a modified AggregatorHost.dll to locate and hijack live Steam sessions, and exfiltrated data to 120.48.156[.]17. iRhythm Holdings disclosed a data breach involving patient personal and health information stored in third-party-hosted business applications. The company said a threat actor contacted it on June 9, 2026, claimed possession of sensitive data, and demanded payment to prevent disclosure. iRhythm attributed access to social engineering and said it had no evidence that products, clinical or medical device systems, patient safety, manufacturing and distribution, or financial reporting systems were affected.
entry.summary: During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally...

entry.title: Mitiga Skillgate Finds AI Agent Instruction File Abuse and 1,230 Leaking Keys
entry.content: This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt exfiltration, model endpoint hijacking, permission bypasses, and exposed credentials. Mitiga Labs published research based on a six-month investigation into AI agent artifacts such as Cursor rules, Anthropic Skills, Claude Hooks, AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, MCP server configurations, package.json droppers, and hidden .pyc bytecode files. The findings indicate that files intended to guide or configure AI agents can also carry techniques associated with spyware, credential exposure, execution abuse, and supply-chain manipulation. The research identified prompt-exfiltration tradecraft, attacker-controlled ANTHROPICBASEURL overrides, and permission-bypass defaults embedded in AI instruction and configuration files. The ANTHROPICBASEURL behavior is particularly notable because it can redirect model traffic toward attacker-controlled infrastructure when agent environments honor the variable, creating an opportunity for interception or manipulation of AI workflows. Mitiga also reported finding more than 1,230 hardcoded API keys and JWT tokens across the examined public repository content. The company said it manually verified in-the-wild findings and did not access or authenticate with the discovered credentials, framing the exposure as part of a broader pattern of sensitive data and control logic being embedded in artifacts consumed by AI coding assistants and agentic systems. Alongside the research, Mitiga Labs released Skillgate, a free scanner designed to detect risky patterns across AI instruction files, skills, hooks, agent brain files, MCP configurations, supply-chain droppers, and bytecode artifacts. According to Mitiga, Skillgate has scanned more than 50,000 files across over 7,000 public repositories. The tool currently includes more than 80 detection rules organized into technique families including direct execution, prompt manipulation, MCP server poisoning, supply-chain attacks, obfuscation, and credential exposure. This rule structure reflects how AI agent ecosystems are beginning to develop their own recognizable abuse patterns, overlapping with traditional malware and software supply-chain techniques while adding AI-specific vectors such as prompt manipulation and agent instruction poisoning. The reporting highlights a shift in attacker opportunity from conventional source code alone to the broader operational context surrounding AI-assisted development. As AI agents increasingly ingest repository-level instructions and configuration files, those artifacts become part of the executable or decision-influencing environment, expanding the set of files that may affect developer workflows, automation behavior, and sensitive credential handling.
entry.summary: This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt...

entry.title: UK Plans Under-16 Social Media Ban and Age Assurance Rules
entry.content: The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance, age assurance, child safety controls, and privacy debates. The UK government plans to ban children under 16 from using user-to-user social media platforms, including TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, X, and YouTube. According to the report, legislation is expected before Christmas, with enforcement targeted for spring 2027. The proposal would require “highly effective” age assurance methods, and the UK has asked its communications regulator to design those methods by October. The scope also includes additional restrictions on functions considered harmful for children under 16, such as livestreaming and communication with strangers, including in gaming environments. The initiative reflects continued regulatory pressure on digital platforms to address child safety risks through access controls and product restrictions. Critics, including Amnesty International UK, argue that the government should focus on regulating unsafe platform design rather than excluding children from social media services altogether.
entry.summary: The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance,...

entry.title: UNC6508 Google Workspace Abuse, SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558, and DPRK Developer Tool Malware
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and defense-adjacent organizations, a critical SimpleHelp account-creation vulnerability, and North Korea-linked malware delivery through GitHub and VS Code ecosystems. Google’s Threat Intelligence Group attributed a campaign targeting North American medical, academic, and military research networks to the China-linked cluster UNC6508. The attackers reportedly compromised externally facing REDCap servers, deployed the INFINITERED backdoor, harvested credentials, and used internal reconnaissance to move deeper into victim environments, including obtaining domain administrator access. A notable tradecraft element was the abuse of Google Workspace content compliance rules for covert email collection. UNC6508 configured rules to silently BCC messages matching nearly 150 keywords, search terms, and email addresses to an attacker-controlled Gmail account, affecting organizations in the United States and Canada including clinical providers, academic centers, military health institutions, advocacy groups, and health regulators. SimpleHelp disclosed CVE-2026-48558, a critical vulnerability affecting versions 5.5.15 and older and 6.0 pre-release builds in specific OpenID Connect configurations. The flaw allows unauthenticated attackers to create privileged Technician accounts without completing multi-factor authentication, potentially enabling remote access to managed endpoints and script execution through the remote support platform. The vulnerability applies where OIDC authentication is enabled, at least one Technician Group is associated with the OIDC provider, and “Allow group authenticated logins” is enabled for that group. SimpleHelp released fixed versions 5.5.16 and 6.0RC2 on June 9, while Horizon3.ai noted that organizations can look for suspicious authenticated technician users and related server log entries; neither SimpleHelp nor Horizon3.ai reported active exploitation. Proofpoint separately reported a North Korea-linked campaign tracked as UNKDeadDrop that sent more than 250 phishing emails over six weeks to individuals at nearly 100 organizations, primarily in the United States. The campaign used developer recruitment, code review, technical assignment, and cryptocurrency project themes to lure recipients into cloning actor-controlled GitHub repositories and opening them in VS Code or Cursor. The malicious repositories abused IDE project behavior, including the “runOn: folderOpen” technique, to execute operating system-specific loaders for macOS, Linux, and Windows without additional user interaction. The malware installed a VS Code extension masquerading as a Google service and supported remote command execution, reconnaissance, and theft of browser wallet extension data, credentials, and desktop wallet application data; Yeeth Security also identified malicious VS Code marketplace extensions posing as Jupyter Notebook tools and using SharePoint and Microsoft Graph API for command-and-control and exfiltration. The U.S. Department of Justice seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, alleging they hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos. The DOJ said the sites shared sexually explicit digital forgeries depicting identifiable people across multiple countries, including politicians, celebrities, athletes, musicians, royalty, journalists, television presenters, entertainers, and others. The seizures were carried out with Homeland Security Investigations after a federal judge found probable cause that the domains were being used to violate the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The investigation began after Italy’s Postal and Cybersecurity Police alerted U.S. authorities, and French investigators later arrested a suspect in Nice, France, on June 10. Maine’s attorney general’s office took its public data breach reporting portal offline after fake breach notices were posted for VRChat and Discord. The office removed the hoax notices and said it had no knowledge of legitimate recent breach reports from either company, while VRChat said the false notice used fake company letterhead and remained online for several hours before removal.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and...

entry.title: CVE-2026-20253, Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day, LiteLLM Chain, and AI Data Exposure Risks
entry.content: This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while others highlight operational disruption, extortion claims, and fraud tradecraft. Splunk Enterprise CVE-2026-20253 remains a high-priority issue for affected 10.0.x and 10.2.x deployments. The unauthenticated network vulnerability stems from missing authentication in a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint, enabling arbitrary file creation or truncation; researchers demonstrated a path to remote code execution by abusing PostgreSQL’s loexport function to write and execute malicious scripts. Splunk Enterprise 10.4.x and Splunk Cloud Platform are reported as unaffected, while Splunk stated that fixed versions are 10.2.4 and 10.0.7 and that no mitigations or workarounds exist. Cisco also released updates for CVE-2026-20262, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw that Cisco says was exploited in zero-day attacks. The vulnerability affects all deployment types and allows a low-privilege remote attacker to execute arbitrary commands as root through crafted HTTP requests to an affected API endpoint during file upload processing. Cisco’s published indicators center on attempts to upload index.jsp and .war files in SD-WAN Manager logs. AI gateway infrastructure is also in scope: Obsidian Security disclosed a three-vulnerability LiteLLM proxy chain rated CVSS 9.9. The chain combines an authorization bypass, privilege escalation via /user/update, and a Custom Code Guardrail sandbox escape, allowing a default low-privilege internaluser to become admin and execute server-side code. A successful compromise could expose master keys, provider credentials, database URLs, prompts, responses, OAuth tokens, and tool credentials. Varonis detailed SearchLeak, a one-click Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search attack path assigned CVE-2026-42824. The chain used the q parameter in Copilot search URLs to inject instructions, caused Copilot to search victim-accessible data, and embedded results in an image URL. A rendering race condition and use of Bing Search by Image as a server-side exfiltration proxy bypassed intended guardrails and Content Security Policy controls; Microsoft mitigated the flaw on the backend, and Varonis reported no observed exploitation. Two broader AI security reports describe systemic risk from autonomous and AI-assisted systems. Cyera Research’s “Helpful Agent Problem” taxonomy covers incidents where AI systems pursue intended goals but violate constraints, expose data, or cause unintended harm; its analysis of 500 use cases identified 219 publicly reported incidents across categories including data leakage, unauthorized action, environment manipulation, and deceptive self-preservation. Separately, security leaders from Datadog, Jamf, and ASOS described AI-assisted “vibe coding” as a driver of code sprawl, with a RedAccess report identifying 380,000 publicly accessible assets across platforms such as Lovable, Base44, and Netlify, including roughly 5,000 containing sensitive corporate information. Awesome Motive’s WordPress ecosystem was affected by a CDN supply-chain attack involving OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage. Sansec reported that malicious scripts were served to OptinMonster and TrustPulse users during a brief Friday window, while PushEngage continued serving malicious JavaScript until Saturday evening UTC. The malware activated when a WordPress administrator visited an infected site, harvesting authentication tokens and nonces before creating a rogue administrator account. Attackers then installed a self-hiding backdoor plugin with remote access capabilities, including a web shell labeled “WPM File Manager & Shell” and arbitrary PHP code execution. Awesome Motive attributed the CDN access to a stolen API key from a compromised marketing server after exploitation of a known UpdraftPlus WordPress plugin flaw. The Council of Europe is investigating ShinyHunters’ claim that the group stole more than 429,000 documents from multiple departments. The alleged data set includes payslips, personnel files, CVs, HR and payroll records, bank account details, tax and Social Security information, medical records, and other personal data. ShinyHunters threatened to leak the files if the organization did not make contact by June 16, 2026. Russian software company Kaluga Astral disclosed a cyberattack earlier this month that disrupted tax reporting, electronic document management, and other business services for about a week. The company said Russian government agencies are involved in the investigation and reported no evidence that customer data was leaked or compromised, but it did not provide attribution, technical details, or motive. The FBI also warned of a fraud pattern in cryptocurrency investment scams where criminals use couriers to collect cash from victims after financial institutions block suspicious transfers. Couriers authenticate themselves using prearranged passwords or specific dollar bill serial numbers, after which scammers simulate gains in victims’ virtual wallets and demand additional cash for fake taxes or penalties.
entry.summary: This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while...

entry.title: CVE-2026-42824 SearchLeak, ShinyHunters Breaches, Check Point VPN Exploitation, and AUR Supply-Chain Malware
entry.content: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software ecosystems, browser extensions, SaaS platforms, and identity workflows to enable credential theft, data exposure, or stealthy persistence. The most significant technical disclosures centered on Microsoft 365 Copilot SearchLeak, Check Point Remote Access VPN exploitation, LangGraph remote code execution research, and a Linux package supply-chain compromise in the Arch User Repository. In parallel, ShinyHunters-linked breaches and Baltic Sea subsea cable damage proceedings highlighted ongoing risks to institutional data and critical infrastructure. Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a critical vulnerability chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search tracked as CVE-2026-42824. The attack combined parameter-to-prompt injection in the Copilot Search q parameter, an HTML rendering race condition, and a Bing server-side request forgery to bypass Content Security Policy restrictions and silently exfiltrate indexed organizational data after a victim clicked a crafted Microsoft 365 Copilot Search link. In the demonstrated SearchLeak flow, Copilot could be instructed to search a victim’s mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other indexed content, then place extracted data into an image URL. The browser request went to an allowlisted Bing endpoint, which fetched an attacker-controlled URL containing the stolen information; Microsoft patched the issue and assigned it a maximum critical severity rating. Check Point Research also reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability affecting Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol. The same reporting noted exploitable LangGraph flaws, where SQL injection and unsafe deserialization in affected checkpointer components could be chained to achieve remote code execution, with patches issued. ShinyHunters activity featured prominently across multiple reports. The University of Nottingham disclosed that the group accessed its student records system, affecting approximately 454,600 current and former students and exposing contact details, passport numbers, enrollment information, and fee payment records. Infinite Campus also reported a Salesforce-targeted data theft incident attributed to ShinyHunters, affecting more than 137,000 school staff accounts. Have I Been Pwned said the leaked data included names, email addresses, employers, job titles, phone numbers, physical addresses, usernames, and support tickets, while Infinite Campus said it had no evidence that customer databases were compromised. These incidents reflect a broader pattern of attackers targeting SaaS-hosted business records and support systems rather than only core production databases. The data described in both cases included personally identifiable information and institutional records useful for downstream phishing, impersonation, and social engineering. Researchers reported a compromise in the Arch User Repository in which attackers modified hundreds of packages to install credential-stealing malware and, where administrative privileges were available, an eBPF rootkit on Linux systems. The campaign highlights the risk of trusted community-maintained package ecosystems being abused for both userland credential theft and deeper kernel-level persistence. A separate browser ecosystem investigation identified 152 Chrome extensions posing as new tab live wallpaper add-ons with a combined 105,000 installs. Socket linked the extensions to adware and traffic-attribution fraud, noting discrepancies between Chrome Web Store claims that user data was not collected and privacy policies describing logging of IP addresses, ISPs, click counts, and referrers. Phishing and credential capture also appeared in targeted email reporting from IRONSCALES, which described a PDF-based lure sent to a security company CEO with a fabricated email thread and a “Review and Sign” button. The message passed DKIM but failed DMARC strict alignment because the signing domain did not align with the header From domain; Microsoft assigned SCL=5 and quarantined it. Finnish prosecutors charged the captain and bosun of the cargo vessel Fitburg over alleged damage to two subsea telecommunications cables and attempted damage to eight other Baltic Sea connections. Authorities said the vessel dragged a damaged anchor along the seabed for at least 130 kilometers, while the defendants deny wrongdoing and are expected to challenge Finland’s jurisdiction. OT risk reporting focused on the difficulty of prioritizing vulnerabilities in industrial environments where devices may remain operational despite serious exposure. The coverage emphasized that long CVE lists and technical alert rankings often fail to convey which weaknesses could affect production, safety, or quality in a specific plant environment. AI governance also surfaced as Anthropic said it disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity-focused models after a U.S. government export control directive barred foreign nationals from accessing them. Anthropic said the government appeared concerned about a jailbreak method but provided only verbal evidence, while the company characterized the identified issues as minor, previously known, and reproducible in other public models.
entry.summary: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software...
